data.title: Aris Mining reports 22% jump in 2025 gold production, guides higher for 2026
data.description:
• Aris Mining (ARMN) on Wednesday reported full-year 2025 gold production of 256,503 ounces, up 22% from the prior year, and said output is expected to climb to 300,000–350,000 ounces in 2026, driven by the ongoing ramp-up at Segovia and the expansion of the Marmato operation.
  • The increase was driven by the expanded Segovia mill and above-guidance performance at Marmato. Fourth quarter production of 69,852 oz capped off a year of strong operational execution.
  • Aris Mining (ARMN) said H2 2025 production of 143,088 oz, up 26% over H1 2025, reflects the installation of Segovia's second ball mill in June 2025.
  • The company reported a cash balance of over $390 million at December 31, 2025, net of the $60 million cash used for the Q4 2025 acquisition of the remaining 49% interest in Soto Norte.
  • Looking ahead to 2026, the miner expects consolidated production of 300,000 to 350,000 oz, supported by continued ramp-up at Segovia and increasing production from Marmato from Q4 2026 onwards, where active mining in the wider porphyry areas is already underway, materially de-risking the project.

data.title: Earnings Snapshot: Kinder Morgan tops Q4 estimates; initiates FY26 outlook below forecast
ONEOK ups quarterly dividend by 4% to $1.07 per share
data.description:
• ONEOK (OKE) increased its quarterly  dividend to $1.07 per share, an increase of 4%.
  • This increase results in an annualized dividend of $4.28 per share.
  • The dividend is payable Feb. 13, 2026, to shareholders of record at the close of business Feb. 2, 2026.

data.title: Dr. Reddy's GAAP EPS of ₹14.52, revenue of ₹87.27B
data.description:

data.title: Great Southern Bancorp GAAP EPS of $1.45 beats by $0.11, revenue of $56.35M beats by $1.32M
data.description:
• Great Southern Bancorp press release (GSBC): Q4 GAAP EPS of $1.45 beats by $0.11.
  • Revenue of $56.35M (-0.2% Y/Y) beats by $1.32M.
  • For the quarter ended December 31, 2025, annualized return on average common equity was 10.16%, annualized return on average assets was 1.16%, and annualized net interest margin was 3.70%, compared to 9.76%, 1.00% and 3.49%, respectively, for the quarter ended December 31, 2024.

data.title: Winmark jumps 6% on SmallCap 600 addition, replacing Guess
data.description:
• Winmark (WINA) is set to replace Guess? (GES) in the S&P SmallCap 600 index before trading opens on January 26. 
  • This change stems from Authentic Brands Group and the Rolling Stockholders and related stockholders acquiring Guess? (GES), with the deal nearing completion pending final conditions.
  • WINA stock price climbed about 6% on Wednesday during after-market hours of trading.

data.title: NVE Corporation targets medical device miniaturization with new wafer-level chip scale sensor as distributor sales rebound
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: NVE Corporation (NVEC) Q3 2026

Management View

  • Daniel Baker, CEO, announced "a 23% increase in revenue and an 11% increase in earnings for the third quarter of fiscal 2026 compared to the prior year quarter, driven by broad-based growth across our revenue lines, including defense and nondefense sales as well as distributor and direct channels."
  • Daniel Nelson, Accounting Manager & Principal Financial Officer, stated: "Revenue for the third quarter of fiscal 2026 increased 23% year-over-year. The increase was due to a 16% increase in product sales and a 335% increase in contract R&D revenue."
  • Nelson further shared, "Gross margin for the third quarter of fiscal 2026 was 79% of revenue compared with 84% in the prior year quarter. The decrease in gross margin percentage was due to a less profitable product mix and increased distributor sales for the quarter."
  • Nelson noted, "Total operating expenses decreased 12% for the third quarter of fiscal 2026 compared to the third quarter of fiscal 2025 due to a 9% decrease in R&D expense and a 19% decrease in SG&A."
  • Peter Eames, Vice President of Advanced Technology, explained, "New equipment in the past year has increased our capacity, increased our capabilities and allowed us to do smaller and more precise wafer-level chip scale package parts in-house... We completed installation and calibration of a new equipment cluster in the past quarter in an expanded production area."
  • Eames also announced, "Just yesterday, we announced a new wafer-level chip scale sensor a part that's just 0.65 millimeter square... This tiny size allows for unmatched miniaturization and special sensitivity."
  • Baker highlighted a key customer development: "In the past quarter, we executed an extension to our supplier partnering agreement with Abbott... this extension is for 2 years through December 31, 2027. It provides for price increases for 2026 and 2027."

Outlook

  • Nelson stated, "We currently expect a full year tax rate of 16% to 17% in fiscal 2026 because we expect advanced manufacturing investment tax credits of between $700,000 and $1 million to offset the impact of other tax law changes."
  • Nelson added, "We expect accelerated deductions of previously unamortized research and development expenses to reduce our cash taxes for the full fiscal year ending March 31, 2026 by approximately $1.1 million."
  • Eames shared, "We expect to place new equipment into service by March 31."

Financial Results

  • Nelson reported, "Net income increased 11% to $3.38 million or $0.70 per diluted share from $3.05 million or $0.63 per share."
  • "Operating margin was 60%. Pretax margin was 68% and net margin was 54%."
  • For the first 9 months of fiscal 2026, "total revenue increased 0.4% to $18.7 million from $18.6 million for the 9 months of the prior year as growth in the most recent quarter more than offset year-over-year decreases in the first 2 quarters of the fiscal year."
  • "Net income for the 9 months decreased 8% to $10.3 million or $2.12 per diluted share."
  • Cash flow from operations was $12.2 million in the first 9 months of the fiscal year. Accounts receivable decreased $1.1 million during the first 9 months primarily due to the timing of customer payments. Inventories decreased by $177,000 due to increased product sales.
  • Fixed asset purchases were $2.18 million for the first 9 months of the fiscal year, including $1.05 million in the December quarter. "We substantially completed spending on our 2-year multimillion dollar expansion."

Q&A

  • Jeffrey Bernstein, Cowen Inc.: "So we talked during the quarter about this idea of magnetic navigation in GPS compromised areas and whether your magnetometer sensors were appropriate for that kind of application. Can you just talk a little bit about that and if you made any contact with anybody in the BOW about this?" Eames replied, "We have looked at MagNav... it's one of the things that we keep an eye on in the defense community to see how it evolves."
  • Bernstein: "Do you have a part that you would deem appropriate for that application today or now?" Eames: "Not exactly. MagNav is pretty new... it's not a mature enough technology that we would chase it for example."
  • Unknown Analyst: "Your new equipment up and running in March... Is that pretty much on the expected schedule that you thought?" Baker: "It is... So things are going well, and we were -- it's a complicated piece of -- set of equipment and a complicated process, but our guys have done a great job of getting it done on schedule."
  • Unknown Analyst: "Is there an expectation that, that new equipment will help with new product sales like adding to new revenue and/or... better profitability because it's packaging, right?" Eames: "I think there is a lot of optimism surrounding the technology that we're developing with the new equipment."
  • Unknown Analyst: "Do you guys see the distributors building up inventory?" Baker: "Yes, it is. It's very positive... our distributor sales are picking up and have been through the fiscal year."
  • Bernstein: "Application for rare earth magnets for position sensing? And what kind of traction you've gotten there?..." Baker: "Our sensors are uniquely positioned to use rare earth free ferrite magnets... we have gotten some sales, and we've gotten some interest in both the magnets and in the sensors that go with them."
  • Unknown Shareholder: "Comments you can make on the current quarter, especially... your defense business, which happens to be a little bit more volatile?" Eames: "I think we're optimistic going forward. I think it's safe to say that we'll be returning to somewhat of a more normal flow there if that's of any help."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed optimism about distributor sales recovery and new equipment deployment, but pressed for details on defense business volatility and MRAM opportunities, reflecting a neutral to slightly positive tone.
  • Management maintained a confident and constructive tone throughout, emphasizing on-schedule equipment rollout and robust customer relationships, supported by phrases like "we're pleased with how it's going" and "I think there is a lot of optimism."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management appeared more upbeat, with greater focus on growth catalysts and less on margin pressures or segment volatility.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter featured a more pronounced increase in both revenue and earnings, contrasted with the prior quarter's modest sequential revenue growth and year-over-year declines.
  • Management's tone has shifted from cautious optimism to a more upbeat and confident outlook, highlighting execution of strategic customer agreements and successful equipment expansion.
  • Analysts' questions this quarter centered more on new products and capacity utilization, while last quarter included more supply chain and margin-focused concerns.
  • The strategic focus has evolved from discussion of capability and product development to practical deployment and sales momentum, underpinned by the new wafer-level sensor launch and the Abbott agreement extension.

Risks and Concerns

  • Nelson highlighted that gross margin decreased due to "a less profitable product mix and increased distributor sales for the quarter."
  • The increase in effective tax rate was attributed to "the noncash impact of tax law changes on certain tax deductions this fiscal year."
  • Supply chain improvements and distributor inventory normalization were noted, but no new macroeconomic or operational risks were introduced in this call.

Final Takeaway

NVE Corporation reported strong revenue and earnings growth for the quarter, supported by improved distributor sales, a new 2-year extension with Abbott, and the rollout of advanced wafer-level chip scale sensors. The leadership team highlighted optimism around the impact of newly installed equipment on production capacity and future product launches, signaling confidence in both operational execution and expanded opportunities in medical and industrial markets.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Kinder Morgan projects $19.8Bcf/day LNG feed gas demand in 2026 as backlog advances to $10B
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Kinder Morgan, Inc. (KMI) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Executive Chairman Richard Kinder stated, "We believe our bullish outlook on natural gas demand remains grounded in reality, and we expect to see very strong growth over the rest of this decade and beyond." He emphasized LNG feed gas as the largest and most certain driver, estimating feed gas demand to average 19.8 Bcf per day in 2026, up 19% from 2025, with projections exceeding 34 Bcf per day by 2030.
  • CEO Kimberly Dang highlighted, "We had a fantastic fourth quarter, producing record results for the quarter and the year. Much stronger than we anticipated when we announced our Q3 results." She noted adjusted EBITDA was up 10% and adjusted EPS grew 22% versus the prior year quarter, attributing outperformance primarily to natural gas. Dang also announced the project backlog increased by $650 million to $10 billion, with over $900 million in new projects added and $265 million placed in service. She recognized Tom Martin's upcoming retirement and Dax’s appointment as President.
  • President Thomas Martin reported, "Transport volumes were up 9% in the quarter versus the fourth quarter of 2024, primarily due to increased LNG feed gas deliveries on Tennessee Gas Pipeline." He added, "Natural gas gathering volumes were up 19% in the quarter...with the largest impact being from our Haynesville system."
  • CFO David Michels stated, "For the fourth quarter, we generated net income attributable to KMI of $996 million and EPS of $0.45, 49% and 50% above the fourth quarter of 2024. This quarter's results included a gain on an asset sale which we treat as a certain item. Excluding certain items, our adjusted net income and adjusted EPS still grew very nicely, both 22% above the fourth quarter of 2024."

Outlook

  • Richard Kinder projected LNG feed gas demand to average 19.8 Bcf per day in 2026, a 19% increase from 2025, and anticipated demand exceeding 34 Bcf per day by 2030.
  • Kimberly Dang stated, "We had a great quarter and year. We also strengthened our balance sheet and advanced key projects with a $10 billion backlog and tremendous potential beyond that we are set up for a very exciting future." She noted Wood Mac projects an incremental 20 Bcf a day of demand growth between 2030 and 2035.
  • The company highlighted over $10 billion in project opportunities beyond the current backlog, with ongoing development in power generation and LNG-related infrastructure.

Financial Results

  • For Q4 2025, adjusted EBITDA rose 10% and adjusted EPS increased 22% compared to Q4 2024. Net income attributable to KMI was $996 million and EPS was $0.45, both significantly higher than the prior year quarter.
  • The company declared a quarterly dividend of $0.2925 per share, annualized at $1.17, up 2% from 2024.
  • The project backlog grew to $10.0 billion, with $3.7 billion in projects added during 2025 despite placing $1.8 billion into service.
  • Net debt to adjusted EBITDA ratio improved to 3.8x from 3.9x in the previous quarter and 4.1x at Q1 2025.
  • Cash flow from operations was $5.92 billion, with $2.6 billion spent in dividends, $3.15 billion in total CapEx, $650 million on the Outrigger acquisition, and $380 million received from divestitures.

Q&A

  • Julien Dumoulin-Smith, Jefferies: Asked about data center demand and regional exposure. Kimberly Dang responded, "About 60% of our backlog is associated with power projects. That's not just data center, that's anything associated with power," citing Georgia Power's projected 53 GW of power demand growth.
  • Jacqueline Koletas, Goldman Sachs: Inquired about Western Gateway capital allocation. Dang explained project returns are "significantly above our cost of capital," and funding is feasible alongside natural gas projects.
  • Koletas followed up on leverage. Dang replied, "We have the ability to fund that 100% out of cash flow...our debt-to-EBITDA actually declines over time."
  • Theresa Chen, Barclays: Queried Western Gateway's EBITDA impact and HH conversion. Dang said it's "too early to go through that at this point" for EBITDA and expects Phase 1 of HH conversion "to come on probably late first quarter, early second quarter."
  • Michael Blum, Wells Fargo: Asked about Bakken exposure post-Continental Resources exit. Dang stated, "EBITDA that we get from Bakken or EBDA, it's about 3% of Kinder Morgan overall...we don't think that there's going to be any material impact from the Continental news."
  • Jeremy Tonet, JPMorgan: Sought views on Waha egress and winter weather impacts. Sital Mody indicated supply/demand dislocations could present opportunities but noted, "this storm is not a Uri."
  • Jean Ann Salisbury, BofA: Questioned permitting timeline improvements. Dang clarified, "the FERC process only took 12 months and we don't have 871 is speeding up our in-service on MSX."
  • Keith Stanley, Wolfe Research: Asked about CapEx guidance. Dang said, "it's largely based on the $10 billion approved project backlog, but there is some view...based on getting some of the $10 billion in the opportunity set."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on growth drivers, project timing, and exposure to specific regions and customers, with a tone that was inquisitive and occasionally cautious, especially regarding project returns and regional demand volatility.
  • Management maintained a confident and upbeat tone, emphasizing strong financial results, robust project backlog, and a positive outlook. Dang frequently referenced "attractive opportunities" and "very nice growth."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management displayed heightened optimism, with management more assertive about balance sheet strength and project execution.
  • Direct expressions of confidence included, "We believe our bullish outlook on natural gas demand remains grounded in reality," and "We are set up for a very exciting future."

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company reported stronger-than-anticipated results in Q4 vs. Q3, with adjusted EBITDA and EPS growth accelerating.
  • Project backlog expanded from $9.3 billion in Q3 to $10 billion in Q4, indicating continued success in sanctioning new projects.
  • Management's tone was more confident in Q4, citing rating upgrades and record financial results, while analysts maintained focus on project returns and capital allocation.
  • Q4 featured more explicit discussion of LNG demand projections and the impact on midstream assets.
  • The previous quarter's focus on shadow backlog opportunities and competitive positioning shifted in Q4 to execution and near-term financial outperformance.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management flagged the need for successful project execution and regulatory approval as ongoing risks, but noted, "there's still a lot of work ahead, but all 3 projects are on budget and on or ahead of schedule."
  • Analysts raised concerns about customer concentration in the Bakken and the impact of Continental Resources' operational changes, but management stated the impact would be "very manageable."
  • Demand volatility, weather impacts, and potential delays in customer off-take were noted, but management expressed confidence in contract structures and market opportunity.

Final Takeaway

Kinder Morgan closed 2025 with record financial performance, a strengthened balance sheet, and a $10 billion project backlog supported by robust natural gas demand and expanding LNG feed gas needs. Management projects continued growth into 2026, underpinned by high contract coverage and a strong opportunity set, positioning the company for further momentum as it advances key infrastructure projects and navigates evolving market dynamics.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Trump administration to offer states deals to host nuclear waste - Reuters
data.description:
The Trump administration will seek interest from U.S. states as soon as this week on storing nuclear waste in return for incentives to build nuclear reactors, Reuters reported Wednesday, but the Department of Energy said no decisions have been made.

Overcoming local opposition to waste storage is considered critical to achieving the administration's ambitious nuclear expansion goals, which include quadrupling U.S. nuclear power capacity to 400 GW by 2050 to help meet surging electricity demand.

Radioactive and toxic nuclear waste currently is kept onsite at nuclear power plants, first in spent fuel pools and then in concrete and steel casks.

A plan several years ago had focused on Nevada's Yucca Mountain as a single repository for all U.S. waste, but former President Obama stopped the project due to local opposition.

President Trump touted nuclear power at the World Economic Forum in Davos on Wednesday, saying the fuel can be developed at "good prices" and be safe despite his own previous concerns.

Trump's remarks helped lift most nuclear-related stocks, as Cameco (CCJ), Denison Mines (DNN), Uranium Energy (UEC), NuScale Power (SMR), Oklo (OKLO), Lightbridge (LTBR), Nano Nuclear Energy (NNE), NexGen Energy (NXE) and BWX Technologies (BWXT) all finished higher. 

ETFs: (NLR), (URA), (URNM)

data.title: Knight-Swift outlines Q1 2026 adjusted EPS guidance of $0.28–$0.32 amid cost initiatives and market tightening
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Knight-Swift Transportation Holdings Inc. (KNX) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Adam Miller stated that truckload market demand was generally stable but did not experience the typical seasonal lift until late in the quarter, with project activity winding down quickly in early November and lower truckload volumes than expected. He noted, "While we did see some improvement in overall demand and a tightening spot market in December, it was a reduction in available capacity that seemed to be the primary driver of the tightening market."
  • Miller emphasized cost discipline, sharing, "Our progress on structurally cutting costs out of the business helped us overcome a $125 million decline in truckload revenue, excluding fuel surcharge, but grew adjusted operating income $28 million in this segment."
  • The CEO highlighted that the company is investing in technology, including AI, to facilitate efficiency gains and expects benefits to be realized in 2026. He also discussed the company's largest fleet in the truckload industry and noted that "one-way service is what typically improves first and most in a tightening market."
  • Miller outlined strategic changes in the LTL segment, noting a more deliberate pace of network expansion to restore margins, and said, "We believe the existing infrastructure has capacity to support annualized revenue of $2 billion."
  • Regulatory and market developments are expected to reduce capacity and favor Knight-Swift's position. Miller pointed to FMCSA and DOT enforcement as a significant industry development in 2025.
  • CFO Andrew Hess disclosed, "Our GAAP results for the current quarter include $52.9 million of noncash impairment charges, primarily related to our decision during the quarter to combine our Abilene Truckload brand into our Swift business."
  • Hess reported, "Adjusted EPS was $0.31 for the fourth quarter of 2025 compared to $0.36 for the fourth quarter of 2024."

Outlook

  • The company projects adjusted EPS for the first quarter of 2026 in the range of $0.28 to $0.32, assuming stable conditions and typical seasonality. CFO Hess stated, "In general, this guidance for the first quarter assumes current conditions remain stable and that we experienced some seasonal slowing in the truckload market and seasonal recovery in the LTL market."
  • Management expects a strong bounce back in the "all other segments" category after a seasonal low, with a significantly reduced range for expected gain on sale due to secondary equipment market trends.
  • CEO Miller indicated that while constructive conversations on rates are occurring, the full benefit from bid season is expected to materialize later in the year, with early progress in shifting volume from brokers to assets as capacity exits the market.

Financial Results

  • Revenue excluding fuel surcharge decreased slightly by 40 basis points, and operating income declined by $51.5 million year-over-year, largely due to the impairment charges.
  • The company reported a GAAP loss per diluted share of ($0.04) for Q4 2025, compared to $0.43 in the prior-year quarter. Adjusted EPS was $0.31, down from $0.36 in Q4 2024.
  • The consolidated adjusted operating ratio was 94%, up 30 basis points year-over-year and 20 basis points sequentially. The effective tax rate on non-GAAP results was 23.1%.
  • In the Truckload segment, revenue excluding fuel surcharge declined 2.4% and adjusted operating income declined $9.2 million year-over-year, primarily due to a 3.3% decline in loaded miles. Revenue per loaded mile increased 0.7% year-over-year and improved 1.4% sequentially.
  • The LTL segment saw revenue excluding fuel surcharge grow 7% year-over-year, with shipments per day up 2.1%. Adjusted operating income decreased 4.8%; adjusted operating ratio increased by 60 basis points year-over-year.
  • Logistics revenue declined 4.8% year-over-year, with a gross margin of 15.5%, down 230 basis points from Q3 and 180 basis points year-over-year. Intermodal adjusted operating ratio improved 140 basis points year-over-year to 100.1%.
  • The "all other segments" category saw revenue increase 17.7%, and the operating loss improved $5.9 million year-over-year.

Q&A

  • Richa Talwar, Deutsche Bank: Asked why the Q1 outlook isn't more robust despite tailwinds and about seasonality. CEO Miller responded that Q1 may not reflect the benefits from bid season, with improvements more likely in later quarters, and noted, "I think the first quarter, we may be in a period where we feel better than we look in terms of the results."
  • Jonathan Chappell, Evercore ISI: Queried whether margin improvement will be driven by cost or require market inflection. CEO Miller said, "We really want to see both ... but if the market doesn't play out on the revenue side ... we're going to go after as much as we can on the cost side to improve margins."
  • Brian Ossenbeck, JPMorgan: Asked about LTL market softness and network expansion. CEO Miller explained that early October saw a demand shift downward, but the expanded network opens new bidding opportunities. CFO Hess added unified branding is enhancing sales efforts.
  • Ravi Shanker, Morgan Stanley: Inquired about brand strategy around acquisitions and the impact of combining Abilene into Swift. CEO Miller detailed the decision and said it was unique to Abilene's situation.
  • Daniel Moore, Robert W. Baird: Asked about the cost-out strategy and sustainability. CEO Miller and CFO Hess outlined ongoing and future cost initiatives, including technology deployment and facility rationalization.
  • Christian Wetherbee, Wells Fargo: Probed shippers' urgency and potential driver wage increases. CEO Miller noted some shippers are acting proactively, and driver wages may be handled dynamically based on market conditions.
  • Ken Hoexter, BofA Securities: Sought clarity on whether early January improvements are demand or capacity-driven. CEO Miller attributed improved network balance mainly to capacity tightening.
  • Scott Group, Wolfe Research: Questioned the relationship between price, utilization, and margin. CEO Miller said the aim is to achieve both price and utilization improvements in the next cycle.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed management for clarity on the outlook, margin sustainability, and market inflection timing, with a tone that was generally neutral but occasionally skeptical, particularly regarding seasonality and the durability of cost improvements.
  • Management maintained a neutral to slightly positive tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, with confidence in cost-cutting and technology initiatives, but hedged optimism about market recovery. CEO Miller used phrases like "we believe," "we're encouraged," and "we feel better," indicating some caution but growing confidence compared to previous quarters.
  • Compared to Q3, analyst questions remained focused on margin progression and market inflection, but management's confidence appeared to strengthen, particularly in discussing permanent cost reductions and readiness for a market upturn.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q4 guidance was more cautious, emphasizing seasonality and the timing of bid season impacts, compared to Q3's focus on proactive customer discussions and early bid activity.
  • There was a continued strategic shift toward technology-enabled efficiency, with new emphasis on AI and unified branding in LTL.
  • Management highlighted permanent cost reductions as a critical differentiator this quarter, with more explicit discussion of structural improvements and technology investments.
  • Analyst questions in both quarters centered on capacity, cost, and pricing, but Q4 saw more focus on network integration and the impact of regulatory actions on capacity.
  • Key metrics such as adjusted EPS and operating ratio deteriorated in Q4, largely due to impairment charges, while revenue growth in LTL moderated.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited continued softness in truckload and LTL demand as a risk.
  • Secondary equipment market weakness and falling average prices were noted.
  • Increased regulatory enforcement and capacity exits were identified as both risks and potential tailwinds for the company's competitive position.
  • The rise in cargo theft and the impact on the logistics segment were highlighted, with mitigation efforts focused on tightening carrier qualifications and leveraging asset-based relationships.
  • Analysts expressed concern about timing of demand recovery, sustainability of cost cuts, and the impact of regulatory actions on labor and capacity.

Final Takeaway

Knight-Swift management emphasized that despite a challenging demand environment and impairment-related losses in Q4 2025, the company is positioned for earnings growth through permanent cost reductions, a more integrated and tech-enabled network, and anticipated market tightening in 2026. The company projects Q1 2026 adjusted EPS of $0.28 to $0.32, reflecting a cautious outlook until bid season and market improvements materialize later in the year. Management remains focused on leveraging its scale and operational efficiency to capture emerging opportunities as market conditions evolve.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
